title: Wall-Mounted Tool Cabinet
summary: French-cleated hanging cabinet with doors. Every tool visible, nothing in a drawer where it dies.
description:
Tools in drawers get forgotten. Tools on a wall get used. This cabinet puts your hand tools at eye level behind two doors that themselves hold tools.

The French cleat is the whole mounting system: a 45° bevel on a strip screwed to the studs, a matching bevel on the cabinet, and the cabinet just drops on. It's strong, it's self-aligning, and it lets you take the cabinet down in one motion.

Build the carcass, then design the tool holders **around the tools you actually own** — not the ones in the photo.

tools.note: The 45° cleat bevels. Set the blade once, cut both halves, guaranteed match.
A level, for hanging the cleat.

materials.name: Hardwood, 3/4", for the French cleat
materials.note: Hardwood, not ply. This strip carries the entire loaded cabinet.

materials.name: Piano hinge or butt hinges
materials.note: Loaded doors are heavy. Piano hinge distributes the load and won't sag.

materials.name: Lag screws, 3", for mounting into studs
Clear interior finish (hardwax oil or wipe-on poly)
materials.note: A light coat inside makes it easy to wipe sawdust out.

cutList.note: Each door is a shallow tray — it holds tools too. That's half the cabinet's capacity.
45° bevel along the top edge, pointing UP and toward the wall.
The mating 45°, pointing DOWN and toward the wall. Cut both from one setup.

steps.title: Build the carcass
steps.body:
Rabbet the sides to receive the top and bottom, glue and screw. Rabbet the back edge for the 1/2" back panel.

Glue and screw the back in — it squares the case and it's what the cabinet cleat screws into. Check the diagonals.

steps.title: Cut both French cleat halves in one setup
steps.body:
Tilt the table saw blade to 45°. Rip both cleat pieces **without changing the setting.**

One setup guarantees the two bevels mate perfectly. Cut them separately and you'll get 44° and 46°, and the cabinet will hang with a gap at the top.

steps.title: Mount the cabinet cleat
steps.body:
Screw the cabinet's cleat to the back panel, near the top, bevel pointing **down and toward the wall**, so it hooks over the wall cleat.

Use plenty of screws into the back and into the carcass sides. Everything the cabinet weighs passes through this joint.

steps.title: Build the door trays
steps.body:
Each door is a shallow frame — a plywood panel with a 3" lip around it — so it can hold tools on its inside face.

This effectively doubles your storage. Doors that are just flat panels are a wasted opportunity.

steps.title: Hang the doors
steps.body:
Piano hinge each door to the carcass sides. Test the swing before you load anything — a loaded door is heavy, and a hinge that binds empty will tear out full.

Add magnetic catches.

steps.title: Hang the wall cleat — into studs
steps.body:
Find your studs. Lag the wall cleat into **at least two of them**, bevel pointing up and toward the wall. Level it carefully — the cabinet hangs exactly as level as this strip.

Drywall anchors are not an option here. A loaded tool cabinet is 60+ lbs and it will come off the wall.
materials: Lag screws, 3", for mounting into studs

steps.title: Hang it, then design the holders
steps.body:
Lift the cabinet and drop it onto the cleat. It self-aligns and locks under its own weight. This is the satisfying moment.

Now lay your actual tools on the shelves and doors, shuffle them until the arrangement makes sense, and **then** build the holders — sawn kerfs for chisels, dowels for saws, blocks for planes.

Building holders before you've arranged the tools is how you end up with a beautiful cabinet that doesn't fit your tools.
materials: Clear interior finish (hardwax oil or wipe-on poly)
